macro_events.event_summary: Japan's April core inflation came in softer than expected, reducing the likelihood of a near-term Bank of Japan rate hike and pressuring the yen lower.
primary_drivers: Weaker yen vs USD from delayed BOJ tightening boosts Japanese exporter earnings translated to USD ADRs
Wider US-Japan rate differential supports carry trade and risk assets
Lower Japanese yields keep Japanese institutional capital flowing into US Treasuries and equities

beneficiaries.rationale: Toyota ADR — weaker yen directly inflates yen-denominated export revenue when translated; every 1 yen of JPY weakness vs USD historically adds roughly ¥45bn to operating profit. Delayed BOJ hike extends this tailwind.
Honda ADR — similar FX translation mechanic as Toyota; majority of profits earned overseas but reported in yen, so yen weakness lifts reported earnings.
Counter-intuitive but ADR tracks Tokyo shares which rally on weak-yen/export theme; offset by lower NIM expectations from delayed hike makes this only medium confidence.

headlines: Japan inflation softens more than expected in April, weakening case for BOJ rate hike

macro_events.event_summary: Asia-Pacific markets poised to open higher as investors digest progress on U.S.-Iran peace deal diplomacy, implying lower geopolitical risk premium and softer oil.
primary_drivers: Lower geopolitical risk premium in crude oil (Iranian barrels potentially returning to market)
Compressed Middle East tail risk reduces defense spending urgency narrative
Risk-on rotation favoring cyclicals, EM, and oil-consuming industries

beneficiaries.rationale: Jet fuel is ~20-25% of operating costs; a sanctions-relief-driven drop in Brent directly expands airline margins. Delta has historically shown highest beta to crude moves among US legacy carriers.
Same fuel-cost mechanism as DAL; United also has large international exposure that benefits from de-escalation reducing route/insurance costs over Middle East corridors.
Southwest is the least hedged major US carrier on jet fuel in recent years, giving it the most direct P&L leverage to a sustained Brent decline from Iran supply normalization.
Bunker fuel is a top-3 cost line for cruise operators; Carnival has the largest fleet and highest fuel sensitivity. Also benefits from reduced Eastern Mediterranean itinerary disruption risk.
Naphtha/ethane feedstock costs fall with crude; Dow's commodity chemicals margins expand on lower oil. Also a high-beta cyclical that rallies in risk-on tape.

losers.rationale: Direct revenue exposure to Brent realization prices. Iran returning ~1-1.5mbpd of additional barrels to legal market would pressure crude $5-10 lower, hitting upstream EPS sensitivity.
Highest oil-price beta in US large-cap E&P due to leveraged balance sheet and Permian pure-play exposure; every $1 Brent move ~2-3% EPS swing.
Mid-cap E&P with concentrated Permian + Egypt exposure; high operating leverage to crude price decks. Frequently the highest-beta name on oil down-days.
Patriot/THAAD/missile demand narrative weakens with Middle East de-escalation; Lockheed has the most direct exposure to Iran-deterrence-linked munitions and missile defense backlogs.
Raytheon missile segment (Standard Missile, SM-6, Patriot) is core to Gulf allies' Iran-deterrence procurement; peace dividend narrative compresses defense multiple.
Defense peer-rotation loser on de-escalation headlines; less direct Iran exposure than LMT/RTX but caught in sector multiple compression on peace-deal tape.
Oilfield services capex is gated by operator FCF and oil price; sustained Brent decline from Iran supply pressures NAM completion activity and pricing power.

headlines: Asia-Pacific markets set to open higher as investors assess U.S.-Iran peace deal diplomacy
